mcqs.question: What is the worst-case time complexity of Heapsort?

options.text: O(n)
O(n log n)
O(n²)
O(log n)

mcqs.explanation: Heapsort always performs at O(n log n) time because building the heap takes O(n) and each of the n extractions takes O(log n) time.

mcqs.question: Which of the following sorting algorithms is NOT a linear time sorting algorithm (i.e., not Ω(n)?

options.text: Counting Sort
Radix Sort
Bucket Sort
Merge Sort

mcqs.explanation: Merge Sort has a time complexity of Ω(n log n), making it not a linear time sorting algorithm.

mcqs.question: In the heapsort algorithm, what is the first step to sort an array in ascending order?

options.text: Extract the maximum element repeatedly from the heap
Build a max-heap from the unsorted array
Sort the heap using insertion sort
Convert the array into a min-heap

mcqs.explanation: The first step in heapsort is to build a max-heap from the unsorted array, which arranges elements according to the heap property.

mcqs.question: Counting Sort is most efficient when:

options.text: The range of input values (k) is much smaller than the number of elements (n)
The input consists of floating-point numbers
The input is randomly distributed
The elements are in reverse sorted order

mcqs.explanation: Counting Sort runs in O(n + k) time, so it is efficient when k (range of values) is not significantly larger than n.

mcqs.question: In Radix Sort, which digit is processed first when using LSD (Least Significant Digit) approach?

options.text: The most significant digit
The least significant digit
A random digit position
The middle digit

mcqs.explanation: LSD Radix Sort processes digits from the least significant to the most significant, ensuring stability at each pass.

mcqs.question: Bucket Sort achieves O(n) average-case time complexity when:

options.text: The input is uniformly distributed over a known range
All elements are identical
The input is already sorted in reverse order
The input contains only prime numbers

mcqs.explanation: Bucket Sort distributes elements uniformly into buckets; if well-distributed, sorting each bucket (usually with another algorithm) gives O(n) average time.

mcqs.question: Which of the following statements is TRUE about stability in sorting algorithms?

options.text: Heapsort is stable because it maintains the relative order of equal elements
Counting Sort is stable because it uses the counting array to preserve order
Radix Sort using LSD is always unstable
Bucket Sort is always unstable regardless of the bucket sorting method

mcqs.explanation: Counting Sort is stable because when building the output array, we process elements from right to left ensuring equal elements retain their original relative order.

mcqs.question: A database system needs to sort 1 million records by a field containing employee IDs (ranging from 1 to 10,000). Which sorting algorithm would be MOST efficient and WHY?

options.text: Heapsort, because it guarantees O(n log n) worst-case time
Counting Sort, because the range of IDs is much smaller than the number of records, giving O(n + k) time
Quick Sort, because it has the best average-case performance
Merge Sort, because it is naturally parallelizable

mcqs.explanation: With n = 1,000,000 and k = 10,000, Counting Sort runs in O(n + k) ≈ 1,010,000 operations, which is significantly faster than O(n log n) algorithms.
